text:
Morgan Stanley nails it: 

“… Strip away the narrative for a moment and read only the [oil] prices. They describe a market that has weakened across the board…”

CHART OF THE DAY: The global energy system, split by regions and source (from the annual @EnergyInstitute 'Statistical Review of World Energy,' celebrating this year its 75th edition). Do look at that big chunk of grey at the center of the chart — that's coal (China and India). https://t.co/h4jrG6LYaG
The full report is here: https://t.co/7bX5pmrAqU

BP deputy CEO (and head of trading) Carol Howle is retiring 3 months after she was promoted to the deputy job. 

The company says that was always the plan, although BP didn’t indicate it was a short-term appointment when her new title was announced. Sam Skerry takes over as head of trading.

Another awfully weak trading day in the physical oil windows. Even after sellers offered the largest ever discounts for some WAF crudes, buyers are not emerging. In the North Sea, physical premia for some grades hit a fresh 6-year low.

Danish authorities have dropped a high profile case against a group of energy traders for alleged market manipulation. 

The case, dating back to 2023, shocked the close-knit world of electricity and gas trading in Aarhus, the Danish town considered the Silicon Valley of European energy trading. 

https://t.co/XUkxSQ3HxX

US total oil production -- and crude output -- rose to a record high in April, according to revised @EIAgov data.

The monthly data pegs US total oil output at 21.836m b/d in April (up 565,000 b/d from the initial estimate for the month based on weekly data). On an annual basis, US total oil production is up >1m b/d. 

April crude production was revised higher to 13.934m b/d (up 348,000 b/d from the weekly data) while NGLs output was also revised up, to 7.903m b/d (up 218,000 b/d from weekly data).

@ExergyLab @EnergyInstitute I thought the chart above was “great because it “breaks down global energy system” and shows “how dominant fossil fuels still are in providing total energy supply.” But maybe that’s completely wrong.
